summary: The empire used knowledge from many cultures.

headline: Kublai Khan Ruled China
body: Genghis Khan's grandson Kublai Khan established the Yuan Dynasty and ruled China.
summary: The Mongols once ruled China directly.

headline: Many Cultures Lived Under Mongol Rule
body: The empire contained a wide variety of peoples, religions, and civilizations.
summary: The Mongol Empire was incredibly diverse.

headline: Trade Routes Helped Drive Expansion
body: Control over major trade routes was one factor behind Mongol expansion.
summary: Trade was as important as conquest.

headline: The Silk Road Wasn't Just One Road
body: The Silk Road was actually a network of trade routes connecting East and West.
summary: The Silk Road was a web of routes rather than a single path.

headline: The Silk Road Stretched About 4,000 Miles
body: The ancient trade network extended roughly 4,000 miles across Asia.
summary: The route covered thousands of miles.

headline: Few People Traveled the Entire Route
body: Most merchants only traveled parts of the Silk Road, with goods changing hands many times.
summary: Very few people completed the whole journey.

headline: Rome and China Traded Through the Silk Road
body: The Silk Road connected two of the world's greatest civilizations through trade.
summary: Goods moved between Rome and China.

headline: Buddhism Reached China Along the Silk Road
body: Merchants and travelers helped spread Buddhism from India into China.
summary: Trade routes also carried ideas and religions.

headline: Christianity Also Traveled the Silk Road
body: Early Christian communities spread eastward through Silk Road trade networks.
summary: Religions moved alongside goods.

headline: The Black Death May Have Traveled the Silk Road
body: Many historians believe plague bacteria reached Europe through Silk Road trade.
summary: Trade routes may have helped spread one of history's deadliest pandemics.

headline: Marco Polo Traveled the Silk Road
body: Venetian explorer Marco Polo journeyed to China during the era of Mongol rule.
summary: Marco Polo became one of the Silk Road's most famous travelers.

headline: The Mongols Revived the Silk Road
body: Trade along the Silk Road experienced a revival during the Mongol Empire.
summary: Mongol rule brought renewed trade across Asia.

headline: Parts of the Silk Road Still Exist
body: Sections of the ancient Silk Road survive today as modern highways.
summary: Ancient trade routes still influence the modern world.

headline: The French Revolution Ended the Monarchy
body: The French Revolution overthrew France's monarchy and transformed the country's political system.
summary: The French monarchy fell during the Revolution.

headline: The Revolution Lasted More Than a Decade
body: The French Revolution unfolded between 1787 and 1799.
summary: The upheaval lasted about twelve years.

headline: A King Was Executed by Guillotine
body: King Louis XVI was executed in 1793 during the Revolution.
summary: France executed its own king.

headline: Marie Antoinette Was Executed Too
body: Queen Marie Antoinette was executed during the French Revolution.
summary: The queen met the same fate as the king.

headline: The Revolution Spread Democratic Ideas
body: The French Revolution helped popularize ideas of democracy and individual rights around the world.
summary: Its ideas influenced countries far beyond France.

headline: The American Revolution Helped Trigger It
body: France's costly support of the American Revolution contributed to the financial crisis that fueled unrest.
summary: Helping America indirectly hurt France's finances.

headline: The Storming of the Bastille Became a Symbol
body: The capture of the Bastille prison in 1789 became one of the Revolution's defining moments.
summary: A prison became a symbol of revolution.

headline: Thousands Died During the Reign of Terror
body: The Reign of Terror resulted in thousands of executions.
summary: Revolutionaries eventually turned on one another.

headline: Napoleon Rose After the Revolution
body: Napoleon Bonaparte gained power in the aftermath of the French Revolution.
summary: The Revolution helped pave the way for Napoleon.

headline: Nationalism Spread Across Europe
body: The Revolution encouraged nationalist movements throughout Europe.
summary: Its influence reshaped the continent.

headline: Movable Type Existed Before Gutenberg
body: Printing with movable type was being used in Korea before Gutenberg's work in Europe.
summary: Asia pioneered movable type printing centuries earlier.

headline: Gutenberg Printed His Bible Around 1455
body: Johannes Gutenberg completed his famous Bible around 1455.
summary: One of history's most important books appeared in the 1400s.

headline: The Gutenberg Bible Was the First Complete Printed Western Book
body: The Gutenberg Bible is the oldest complete printed book preserved from the Western world.
summary: The first surviving complete Western printed book still exists.

headline: Gutenberg's Design Lasted Centuries
body: The basic design of Gutenberg's press remained largely unchanged for over 300 years.
summary: One invention dominated printing for centuries.

headline: Early Printing Was Surprisingly Slow
body: Gutenberg-era presses produced roughly 250 sheets per hour on one side.
summary: Printing books once took incredible patience.

headline: Gutenberg's Press First Appeared in a Lawsuit
body: The earliest mention of Gutenberg's press comes from legal records in Strasbourg.
summary: One of history's greatest inventions first appears in court documents.

headline: Print Was the Original Mass Media
body: Before radio and television, printed books and newspapers were the main way ideas spread widely.
summary: Mass communication existed long before electronics.

headline: The Oldest Surviving Metal Type Book Is Korean
body: The Korean book Jikji, printed in 1377, predates Gutenberg by 78 years.
summary: The oldest surviving metal type book wasn't European.

headline: Only About 40 Gutenberg Bibles Survive
body: Roughly 40 copies of the Gutenberg Bible still exist.
summary: One of history's rarest books survives in small numbers.

headline: Computers Changed Printing Again
body: Beginning in the 1950s, computers transformed the printing process.
summary: Digital technology revolutionized printing.

headline: The Superpowers Rarely Fought Each Other
body: The Cold War was largely fought without direct military conflict between the United States and the Soviet Union.
summary: The world's biggest rivals mostly avoided shooting at each other.

headline: George Orwell Coined 'Cold War'
body: Writer George Orwell used the term 'Cold War' in 1945 to describe a nuclear standoff between superpowers.
summary: The phrase came from the author of 1984.

headline: The Cold War Lasted More Than 40 Years
body: The Cold War stretched from 1947 until 1991.
summary: An ideological rivalry lasted nearly half a century.

headline: The World Came Close to Nuclear War
body: The Cuban Missile Crisis brought the United States and Soviet Union to the brink of nuclear conflict.
summary: Humanity narrowly avoided catastrophe.

headline: Fear Prevented Nuclear War
body: Both sides avoided using nuclear weapons because each could destroy the other.
summary: Mutual destruction kept the peace.

headline: The Soviet Union Broke America's Monopoly
body: The Soviet Union tested its first atomic bomb in 1949.
summary: America's nuclear advantage didn't last long.

headline: NATO Was Born During the Cold War
body: NATO was established in 1949 to counter Soviet influence in Europe.
summary: One of the world's largest alliances emerged from the rivalry.

headline: A Blockade Was Defeated Without a Battle
body: The Berlin Airlift supplied West Berlin and overcame a Soviet blockade without firing a shot.
summary: Cargo planes defeated a blockade.

headline: Korea Became a Proxy War
body: The Korean War became one of the first major conflicts tied to Cold War tensions.
summary: Other countries fought while superpowers backed each side.

headline: The Soviet Union's Collapse Ended the Cold War
body: The Cold War effectively ended when the Soviet Union dissolved in 1991.
summary: The rivalry ended when one superpower disappeared.

headline: The Space Race Was a Superpower Competition
body: The Space Race pitted the United States against the Soviet Union.
summary: Space became another Cold War battlefield.

headline: Sputnik Was the First Satellite
body: Sputnik 1 became the world's first artificial satellite in 1957.
summary: Humanity's first satellite was Soviet.

headline: The First Human in Space Was Soviet
body: Yuri Gagarin became the first human to travel into space in 1961.
summary: The Soviet Union put the first person into orbit.

headline: The Soviets Won Many Early Victories
body: The Soviet Union achieved many of the Space Race's first milestones.
summary: America started out behind.

headline: Apollo 11 Reached the Moon
body: Apollo 11 successfully landed humans on the Moon in 1969.
summary: Humanity first stepped onto another world in 1969.

headline: The First Moonwalkers Were Armstrong and Aldrin
body: Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin became the first people to walk on the Moon.
summary: Two astronauts made history together.

headline: The Moon Landing Marked America's Victory
body: Apollo 11 is widely considered the symbolic U.S. victory in the Space Race.
summary: The Moon became the ultimate prize.

headline: The Space Race Came From the Cold War
body: Competition in space grew directly from Cold War tensions.
summary: Political rivalry fueled exploration.

headline: Sputnik Alarmed the United States
body: Sputnik's launch shocked Americans and accelerated U.S. investment in space.
summary: One satellite changed history.

headline: Competition Accelerated Technology
body: The rivalry between superpowers rapidly advanced space technology.
summary: Competition helped humanity reach the stars.

headline: Ancient Egypt Lasted Over 3,000 Years
body: Ancient Egyptian civilization endured for more than three millennia.
summary: One civilization survived longer than many entire eras of history.

headline: The Nile Made Egypt Possible
body: Annual flooding of the Nile River supported Egyptian civilization.
summary: Without the Nile, ancient Egypt might never have existed.

headline: The Great Pyramid Is the Oldest Surviving Wonder
body: The Great Pyramid of Giza is the only Ancient Wonder of the World still standing.
summary: People can still visit the oldest surviving Wonder today.

headline: Hieroglyphics Were an Early Writing System
body: Ancient Egyptians developed one of the world's earliest writing systems.
summary: Pictures became words thousands of years ago.

headline: King Tut's Tomb Was Found Intact
body: Howard Carter discovered the nearly untouched tomb of Tutankhamun in 1922.
summary: The discovery shocked the world.

headline: Cats Were Sacred in Ancient Egypt
body: Cats were highly valued and associated with several Egyptian gods.
summary: Ancient Egyptians adored cats.

headline: Egyptians Wrote on Papyrus
body: Papyrus was one of the world's earliest paper-like materials.
summary: Ancient paperwork was made from plants.

headline: Pharaohs Were Seen as Divine
body: Egyptian rulers were considered both kings and divine figures.
summary: Their leaders were treated like gods.

headline: Menes Unified Egypt
body: Tradition credits Menes with uniting Upper and Lower Egypt.
summary: A single ruler brought Egypt together.

headline: Ancient Egypt Became Part of Rome
body: Ancient Egypt eventually became absorbed into the Roman Empire.
summary: Even mighty Egypt was conquered.

headline: The Maya Built Dozens of Cities
body: At their peak, the Maya had more than 40 major cities.
summary: A vast civilization flourished across Central America.

headline: The Maya Discovered Zero
body: The Maya independently developed the mathematical concept of zero.
summary: They solved one of mathematics' greatest ideas.

headline: The Maya Were Skilled Astronomers
body: Maya astronomers accurately tracked the movements of the Moon and Venus.
summary: They watched the heavens with remarkable precision.

headline: The Maya Created Sophisticated Calendars
body: The Maya developed complex calendar systems to track time.
summary: Ancient people measured time with incredible accuracy.

headline: The Maya Used Hieroglyphics
body: The Maya developed a hieroglyphic writing system that has largely been deciphered.
summary: Their symbols tell stories across centuries.

headline: Chichén Itzá Outlived Many Cities
body: Chichén Itzá thrived long after many earlier Maya cities declined.
summary: Some cities survived while others vanished.

headline: Jungles Reclaimed Maya Cities
body: Many abandoned Maya cities were eventually swallowed by jungle vegetation.
summary: Entire cities disappeared beneath forests.

headline: Millions of Maya Descendants Still Live Today
body: Modern Maya communities continue to thrive across Central America.
summary: The Maya civilization never truly disappeared.

headline: The Maya Made Books From Trees
body: Maya codices were made from the bark of wild fig trees.
summary: Ancient books once grew on trees.

headline: Some Maya Cities Held 50,000 People
body: Major Maya cities could contain populations of up to 50,000 inhabitants.
summary: Ancient cities rivaled large towns.

headline: The Aztecs Built a City on a Lake
body: The Aztecs founded Tenochtitlán on an island in Lake Texcoco in 1325. The city would eventually become the heart of their empire.
summary: The Aztecs built their capital on an island in a lake.

body: According to Aztec tradition, their homeland would be marked by an eagle perched on a cactus holding a serpent. That image survives today on Mexico's national flag.
summary: The symbol on Mexico's flag comes from an Aztec legend.

body: At its height, Tenochtitlán had more than 140,000 inhabitants, making it one of the largest cities in the world.

body: By the early 1500s, the Aztec Empire controlled between five and six million people.
summary: Millions lived under Aztec rule.

headline: The Aztecs Built Floating Gardens
body: Aztec farmers created raised fields called chinampas, often nicknamed floating gardens, that allowed them to grow multiple harvests each year.
summary: The Aztecs engineered floating gardens for farming.

headline: The Aztecs Used Two Calendars at Once
body: The Aztecs combined two calendar systems that repeated together every 52 years.
summary: The Aztecs tracked time with an elaborate calendar system.

headline: Nahuatl Became the Language of an Empire
body: Nahuatl became the dominant language across much of what is now Mexico during the Aztec Empire.
summary: One language spread across much of ancient Mexico.

headline: The Aztecs Enjoyed Chocolate Before Europe
body: Chocolate drinks were consumed by the Aztecs centuries before Europeans encountered cacao.
summary: The Aztecs were enjoying chocolate long before Europe discovered it.

headline: Mexico City Was Built on the Aztec Capital
body: Modern Mexico City stands where Tenochtitlán once ruled the Aztec Empire.
summary: Mexico City grew from the ruins of Tenochtitlán.

headline: Human Sacrifice Was Part of Aztec Religion
body: Human sacrifice played an important role in Aztec religious practices and ceremonies.
summary: Religion and sacrifice were closely connected in Aztec society.

headline: The Inca Empire Stretched Across South America
body: At its height, the Inca Empire extended from modern Ecuador to central Chile.
summary: The Inca ruled a huge section of South America.

headline: The Inca Controlled Millions in Just a Century
body: Within about 100 years, the Inca expanded their empire to include roughly 12 million people.
summary: The Inca built a massive empire with incredible speed.

headline: The Inca Built Thousands of Miles of Roads
body: The Inca created a vast road network that crossed mountains and connected their empire.
summary: The Inca engineered roads across the Andes.

headline: Inca Messengers Could Relay News 150 Miles a Day
body: Relay runners carried messages across the empire at speeds of about 150 miles per day.
summary: Information traveled surprisingly fast in the Inca Empire.

headline: The Inca Recorded Information With Knots
body: The Inca used knotted cords called quipu to keep records and track information.
summary: Knots served as a recordkeeping system.

headline: The Inca Built Suspension Bridges
body: Long before Europeans arrived, the Inca constructed suspension bridges to connect distant regions.
summary: Ancient engineers built bridges across mountain valleys.

headline: The Inca Had No Written Language
body: Unlike many ancient civilizations, the Inca left behind no traditional writing system.
summary: One of history's greatest empires had no written language.

headline: Machu Picchu Survives Centuries Later

summary: One of the world's most famous ruins was built by the Inca.

headline: Llamas and Alpacas Were Essential to the Inca

summary: These animals played a major role in daily life.

headline: Millions Still Speak the Language of the Inca
body: Many descendants of the Inca still speak Quechua throughout the Andes today.
summary: The language of the Inca is still alive.

headline: Mesopotamia Is Called the Cradle of Civilization
body: Mesopotamia is often called the cradle of civilization because some of the world's earliest societies emerged there.
summary: Many historians consider Mesopotamia the birthplace of civilization.

headline: Mesopotamia Means 'Between Rivers'
body: The name Mesopotamia comes from Greek and means 'between rivers,' referring to the land between the Tigris and Euphrates.
summary: The civilization's name literally describes its geography.

headline: Two Rivers Helped Create a Civilization
body: Mesopotamian civilization developed between the Tigris and Euphrates Rivers.
summary: Two great rivers supported one of humanity's first civilizations.

headline: Irrigation Turned Dry Land Into Farmland
body: Mesopotamians used irrigation canals to bring water to crops and support growing cities.
summary: Ancient engineers transformed dry land into fertile farmland.

headline: Writing Began in Mesopotamia
body: Mesopotamia gave rise to writing, one of humanity's most important inventions.
summary: One of history's greatest inventions originated here.

headline: Clay Was Used for Almost Everything
body: Mesopotamians used clay for buildings, pottery, and even writing tablets.
summary: Clay shaped nearly every aspect of life.

headline: Mesopotamia Had More Than 1,000 Gods
body: The Mesopotamian pantheon included more than a thousand deities.
summary: Ancient Mesopotamian religion included a huge number of gods.

headline: One of History's Earliest Law Codes Came From Mesopotamia
body: The Code of Hammurabi became one of the earliest and most famous law codes in history.
summary: Ancient Mesopotamia helped shape the idea of written laws.

headline: Your Clock Uses Babylonian Math
body: The 60-minute hour and 60-second minute trace their origins to Babylonian mathematics.
summary: Modern timekeeping still uses ancient ideas.

headline: Mesopotamia Built Giant Temple Towers
body: Mesopotamian cities were famous for massive stepped temples called ziggurats.
summary: Ancient temple towers dominated Mesopotamian skylines.

headline: The Sumerians Built the Earliest Known Civilization
body: Sumer is considered the site of the earliest known civilization in history.
summary: The Sumerians helped launch civilization itself.

headline: The Sumerians Invented Cuneiform
body: The Sumerians developed cuneiform, one of the earliest writing systems ever created.
summary: Their writing changed human history.

headline: Sumer Was Made Up of Many City-States
body: By the third millennium BCE, Sumer consisted of at least twelve separate city-states.
summary: Ancient Sumer was divided into independent cities.

headline: Every City Had Its Own God
body: Each Sumerian city-state worshipped its own patron deity.
summary: Religion shaped life in every city.

headline: The Sumerians Invented the Wheel
body: The Sumerians developed the wheel and the potter's wheel, innovations that transformed transportation and manufacturing.
summary: The wheel traces its origins to ancient Sumer.

headline: Sargon United the City-States

summary: A conqueror brought the region together.

headline: An Ancient King Created One of the Oldest Law Codes
body: Ur-Nammu issued the earliest law code yet discovered in Mesopotamia.
summary: Ancient laws existed long before Rome.

headline: Ancient Kings Were Said to Rule Before a Great Flood
body: The Sumerian King List claimed that eight kings ruled before a great flood.
summary: Flood stories existed in Mesopotamia long before many later traditions.

headline: Ur Was One of Sumer's Greatest Cities
body: The city of Ur became one of the most important centers of Sumerian civilization.
summary: One city stood at the heart of Sumerian culture.

headline: The Sumerians Influenced Future Civilizations
body: Sumerian ideas and institutions shaped many later societies in Mesopotamia.
summary: Their legacy outlived their civilization.

headline: Babylon Was One of History's Greatest Cities
body: Babylon became one of the most famous and powerful cities of the ancient world.
summary: One city became legendary across the ancient world.

headline: Hammurabi Created a Famous Law Code
body: King Hammurabi issued one of history's most famous collections of laws.
summary: Ancient Babylon helped shape the idea of written laws.

headline: Babylonians Counted Using 60

summary: Ancient math still influences daily life.

headline: Your Clock Uses Babylonian Ideas
body: Modern hours and minutes reflect mathematical concepts developed by the Babylonians.
summary: Ancient mathematics lives on in every clock.

headline: Nebuchadnezzar Rebuilt Babylon
body: Nebuchadnezzar II transformed Babylon into one of the most magnificent capitals of the ancient world.
summary: A king turned Babylon into a legendary city.

headline: Babylon Was Home to a Wonder of the World

summary: One of history's most famous wonders was linked to Babylon.

headline: Babylonians Had a Creation Epic

headline: Babylonians Advanced Astronomy
body: Babylonian scholars made major contributions to astronomy and the study of the heavens.

headline: Alexander the Great Wanted Babylon as His Capital
body: Alexander the Great planned to make Babylon the center of his empire.

headline: Babylon Stayed Important for Thousands of Years
body: Babylon remained a major center of culture and power across many centuries.

headline: The Phoenicians Were Master Traders

headline: Phoenicia Was Made Up of Famous Cities

headline: Phoenician Ships Crossed the Mediterranean
body: Phoenician sailors traveled widely across the Mediterranean Sea.
summary: Their ships connected distant civilizations.

headline: Royal Purple Came From the Phoenicians
body: Phoenician merchants traded a valuable purple dye associated with royalty.
summary: Purple once symbolized wealth and power.

headline: Phoenician Craftsmen Were Highly Skilled
body: Phoenician artisans became famous for glasswork, jewelry, and decorative objects.
summary: Their craftsmanship was admired across the ancient world.

headline: The Phoenician Alphabet Influenced Greek Writing
body: The Phoenician alphabet became the foundation of the Greek alphabet.
summary: A writing system changed the future of communication.

headline: Modern Alphabets Trace Back to Phoenicia
body: Through Greek, Phoenician writing influenced many alphabets used today.
summary: Ancient letters still shape modern languages.

headline: Phoenicians Founded Colonies Across the Sea
body: Phoenician settlements spread throughout the Mediterranean world.
summary: They created a vast network of colonies.

headline: Carthage Began as a Phoenician Colony
body: The powerful city of Carthage originally started as a Phoenician settlement.
summary: One famous empire began with Phoenician colonists.

headline: Phoenicians Connected Ancient Cultures
body: Trade routes created by the Phoenicians helped spread ideas and goods across civilizations.
summary: Merchants linked much of the ancient world.

headline: Cyrus the Great Founded One of History's Largest Empires
body: Cyrus the Great established the Persian Empire, which became one of the largest empires the world had ever seen.
summary: Cyrus the Great founded one of history's greatest empires.

headline: The Persian Empire Stretched Across Three Continents
body: At its height, the Persian Empire extended across Asia, Africa, and Europe, making it one of the largest empires of the ancient world.
summary: Ancient Persia once spanned three continents.

headline: Persia Divided Its Empire Into Provinces
body: Darius I organized the empire into provinces called satrapies to help govern its vast territories.
summary: Ancient Persia used provinces called satrapies to manage its empire.

headline: The Persians Built an Ancient Superhighway
body: The Royal Road connected distant parts of the Persian Empire and greatly improved trade and communication.
summary: The Royal Road linked the Persian Empire together.

headline: Persia Had an Ancient Postal Network
body: Mounted couriers carried messages rapidly across the empire using relay stations along the Royal Road.
summary: Ancient Persia operated one of history's earliest communication networks.

headline: Persepolis Was One of the Ancient World's Greatest Cities
body: Persepolis served as a magnificent ceremonial capital filled with impressive palaces and artwork.
summary: Persepolis became one of history's most spectacular cities.

headline: Persian Kings Ruled Many Cultures
body: The Persian Empire governed people from many different cultures, languages, and traditions.
summary: Ancient Persia united diverse peoples under one empire.

headline: Zoroastrianism Began in Ancient Persia
body: One of the world's oldest religions, Zoroastrianism, originated in ancient Persia.
summary: Ancient Persia gave rise to Zoroastrianism.

headline: Alexander the Great Conquered Persia
body: Alexander the Great defeated the Persian Empire and incorporated its territories into his own empire.
summary: Alexander the Great brought the Persian Empire to an end.

headline: The West Called Iran 'Persia' for Centuries
body: For centuries, Western civilizations commonly referred to Iran as Persia, a name derived from the region of Persis.
summary: The name Persia remained widely used for centuries.

headline: The Indus Civilization Covered an Enormous Area
body: The Indus civilization stretched across a larger area than either ancient Egypt or Mesopotamia, making it the most extensive of the world's earliest civilizations.
summary: The Indus civilization was larger than Egypt and Mesopotamia.

headline: The Indus Civilization Was One of Humanity's First
body: The Indus civilization emerged during the third millennium BCE and became one of the world's earliest civilizations.
summary: The Indus civilization ranks among humanity's earliest civilizations.

headline: Two Great Cities Dominated the Indus World
body: Harappa and Mohenjo-daro were the largest and most important cities of the Indus civilization.
summary: Harappa and Mohenjo-daro stood at the center of the Indus civilization.

headline: Indus Cities Were Remarkably Well Planned
body: Indus cities featured carefully designed layouts with straight streets and organized neighborhoods.
summary: The Indus civilization excelled at urban planning.

headline: Many Homes Had Indoor Plumbing
body: Some Indus homes had bathrooms and drainage systems thousands of years before similar features became common elsewhere.
summary: The Indus civilization developed advanced plumbing systems.

headline: The Indus People Used Standard Measurements
body: Archaeologists have found evidence that standardized weights and measures were used throughout the civilization.
summary: The Indus civilization used consistent systems of measurement.

headline: Nobody Has Deciphered the Indus Script
body: Despite decades of study, researchers still cannot fully read the writing system used by the Indus civilization.
summary: The Indus script remains one of archaeology's greatest mysteries.

headline: Archaeologists Have Found Hundreds of Indus Settlements
body: Researchers have discovered hundreds of towns and villages connected to the Indus civilization.
summary: The Indus civilization spread across hundreds of settlements.

headline: Some of the Earliest Cotton Came From the Indus Civilization
body: Archaeologists have discovered some of the oldest known evidence of cotton use in the Indus civilization.
summary: Ancient Indus people were early users of cotton.

headline: Nobody Knows Exactly Why the Indus Civilization Disappeared
body: The decline of the Indus civilization remains uncertain, and historians continue debating the causes.
summary: The disappearance of the Indus civilization remains a mystery.
